reviews.titleZh: Chapter 1 — EMS Systems, Roles, and Responsibilities
reviews.titleEn: Chapter 1 — EMS Systems, Roles, and Responsibilities
reviews.summaryZh: 这章先定义 EMS 系统怎么运作、paramedic 在系统中的定位，以及 profession、scope、medical direction 和 quality improvement 的基本框架。它本质上是在回答：你在 EMS 体系里是谁、你能做什么、你为什么要对自己的行为负责。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter defines how EMS systems function and where the paramedic fits within the system, including professionalism, scope, medical direction, and quality improvement. At its core, it answers: who you are in the EMS system, what you are allowed to do, and why you are accountable for your actions.
highlightsZh: 搞清 EMS system 的组成
理解 paramedic 的角色与责任
知道 scope、accountability 和 medical direction 的边界
highlightsEn: Understand EMS system structure
Define the paramedic role and responsibilities
Know the boundaries of scope, accountability, and medical direction
keyPointsZh: EMS 不是单一救护车，而是一个由 dispatch、现场救援、转运、接收系统、medical oversight 和 quality improvement 组成的完整系统。
paramedic / EMR 的角色不只是“把病人送到医院”，而是要在现场 assessment、initial management、decision-making 和 handoff 中承担专业责任。
scope of practice 决定你能做什么，但 scope 不是“你会什么”，而是“你被授权做什么”。
medical direction 是院前 care 的核心支持系统之一，说明你不是完全独立在做事，而是在一个受监管、受指导的专业体系中工作。
professionalism 不只是态度问题，它会影响病人信任、团队合作、法律风险和系统声誉。
quality improvement 说明 EMS 不是只做任务，而是要持续回顾系统表现、错误和改进空间。
keyPointsEn: EMS is not a single ambulance unit but a full system involving dispatch, scene care, transport, receiving systems, medical oversight, and quality improvement.
The paramedic/EMR role is not just to bring a patient to hospital, but to take professional responsibility for assessment, initial management, decision-making, and handoff.
Scope of practice defines what you are authorized to do; it is not simply what you personally know how to do.
Medical direction is a core support structure in prehospital care, showing that field providers work within a supervised and regulated professional system.
Professionalism is not just about attitude; it affects patient trust, teamwork, legal exposure, and system reputation.
Quality improvement means EMS is not just task performance—it is continuous review, learning, and system refinement.
mustKnowZh: 你必须分清 education / certification / licensure / registration 这些概念，它们都和“能不能执业、在什么边界下执业”有关。
你要理解 roles and responsibilities 不只是工作内容列表，而是 accountability 的来源。
medical direction、documentation、communication 和 system quality 其实从第一章就已经开始了，不是后面才出现的独立话题。
mustKnowEn: You must distinguish concepts such as education, certification, licensure, and registration, because all relate to whether and how you are allowed to practice.
Roles and responsibilities are not just a task list—they are a source of accountability.
Medical direction, documentation, communication, and system quality begin in Chapter 1 conceptually; they are not isolated topics that appear later by accident.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：把 scope of practice 理解成“我会做所以我能做”。实际上，scope 更接近“系统授权你做什么”。
常见误区 2：把 professionalism 当成软性内容。实际上它会直接影响病人安全、团队协作和法律责任。
常见误区 3：以为 quality improvement 是管理层的事。其实 frontline provider 也是 QI 的组成部分，因为你的记录、判断和执行都会进入系统回顾。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: treating scope of practice as ‘if I know how to do it, I can do it.’ In reality, scope is closer to what the system authorizes you to do.
Common confusion 2: viewing professionalism as soft content. In reality, it directly affects patient safety, teamwork, and legal responsibility.

Common confusion 3: thinking quality improvement belongs only to management. In fact, frontline providers are part of QI because their documentation, decisions, and performance feed back into system review.

reviews.titleZh: Chapter 2 — The Well-Being of the Paramedic
reviews.titleEn: Chapter 2 — The Well-Being of the Paramedic
reviews.summaryZh: 这章聚焦急救人员如何长期稳定工作，包括 stress、death and dying、自我保护和职业健康。它真正处理的是：如果你自己先垮掉，后面所有 clinical skill 都没有办法长期稳定发挥。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter focuses on how paramedics stay healthy and functional over time, including stress, death and dying, self-protection, and occupational health. At a deeper level, it asks: if you break down first, none of your clinical skills can be sustained over time.
highlightsZh: 识别职业压力来源
理解 burnout 和 self-protection
知道 emergency vehicle operations 也属于个人安全议题
highlightsEn: Recognize sources of occupational stress
Understand burnout and self-protection
Know that emergency vehicle operations are also personal-safety issues
keyPointsZh: paramedic well-being 不是附属话题，而是职业能力的一部分。
高压、睡眠不足、反复暴露于死亡和创伤，会长期影响判断、反应速度和情绪稳定。
well-being 包含心理、身体、职业和社交层面，不只是“有没有 burnout”。
death and dying 是 EMS 工作的常态风险源之一，不能等真正崩掉后才处理。
self-protection 既包括 PPE 和现场安全，也包括长期压力管理、休息和界限感。
emergency vehicle operations 不只是驾驶技术问题，它本质上也是安全与职业生存问题。
keyPointsEn: Paramedic well-being is not a side topic; it is part of professional capability.
High stress, sleep loss, and repeated exposure to death and trauma can affect judgment, reaction time, and emotional stability over time.
Well-being includes psychological, physical, occupational, and social dimensions—it is more than just burnout.
Death and dying are routine EMS stressors and should be addressed before they become overwhelming.
Self-protection includes PPE and scene safety, but also long-term stress management, rest, and personal boundaries.
Emergency vehicle operations are not just driving issues—they are also safety and professional-survival issues.
mustKnowZh: 你要把 stress management 当成 clinical reliability 的组成部分，而不是“软技能”。
面对死亡、哀伤和 repeated trauma exposure 时，早识别、早处理，比硬撑更专业。
个人安全不是自私，而是 EMS 能不能持续救人的前提。
mustKnowEn: You should treat stress management as part of clinical reliability, not as a ‘soft skill.’
With death, grief, and repeated trauma exposure, early recognition and early response are more professional than simply pushing through.
Personal safety is not selfish—it is a prerequisite for EMS to keep helping others.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：把 well-being 理解成心理健康附加题。其实它会直接影响现场判断和 patient safety。
常见误区 2：认为 burnout 只是“太累了”。实际上 burnout 常常会先表现为冷漠、错误增多、沟通下降和风险容忍度变化。
常见误区 3：觉得 emergency vehicle safety 只是驾驶章节内容。其实它本身就是 paramedic survival 的一部分。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: treating well-being as an optional mental-health add-on. In reality, it directly affects field judgment and patient safety.
Common confusion 2: thinking burnout is just ‘feeling tired.’ In reality, burnout often shows up first as detachment, increased mistakes, worse communication, and changes in risk tolerance.
Common confusion 3: assuming emergency vehicle safety belongs only in a driving chapter. It is actually part of paramedic survival and sustained readiness.

reviews.summaryZh: 这章把 EMS 从事后反应拉向前端 prevention，强调 paramedic 也参与社区健康和 injury prevention。它的核心是在提醒你：真正高水平的 EMS，不只是会处理后果，也要理解如何减少后果发生。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter shifts EMS from purely reactive care toward prevention, showing that paramedics also contribute to community health and injury prevention. Its deeper message is that high-level EMS is not only about handling consequences, but also about understanding how to reduce the chance that those consequences happen at all.
highlightsZh: 理解 prevention 的公共健康价值
认识 EMS 不只是 transport
把社区教育也看成 EMS 角色的一部分
highlightsEn: Understand the public-health value of prevention
Recognize EMS is more than transport
See community education as part of the EMS role
keyPointsZh: EMS 的价值不只在事故后反应，也在于参与 injury 和 illness prevention。
prevention 不是“额外工作”，而是从系统角度减少病人进入 emergency pathway 的方式。
paramedic 在社区中不只是 responder，也可能是 educator、advocate 和 public health participant。
许多可预防伤病背后都有行为、环境和教育因素。
prevention 思维会改变你对 EMS role 的理解：不是只会运送，而是参与系统改善。
keyPointsEn: EMS creates value not only by responding after injury or illness, but also by participating in prevention.
Prevention is not ‘extra work’; it is a systems-level way to reduce how often patients enter the emergency pathway.
In the community, the paramedic may function not only as a responder but also as an educator, advocate, and public-health participant.
Many preventable injuries and illnesses are linked to behavior, environment, and education.
A prevention mindset changes how you understand the EMS role: not just transport, but system improvement.
mustKnowZh: 要知道 prevention 是 EMS identity 的一部分，而不是可有可无的附属项。
要理解公共健康视角会改变你看 injury / illness 的方式。
要记住许多 EMS 接触点，本身就是 prevention opportunity。
mustKnowEn: Know that prevention is part of EMS identity, not an optional side topic.
Understand that a public-health perspective changes how you think about injury and illness.
Remember that many EMS contact points are themselves prevention opportunities.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：觉得 prevention 是社区机构的事，不是 EMS 的事。实际上 EMS 也在 prevention 链条里。
常见误区 2：把 prevention 理解成“讲几句安全建议”。其实它还涉及系统设计、资源连接和风险识别。
常见误区 3：认为 EMS 只有在 emergency 中才有价值。Chapter 3 在打破这个想法。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: thinking prevention belongs only to community agencies and not EMS. In reality, EMS is part of the prevention chain.
Common confusion 2: reducing prevention to simply giving safety advice. In reality, it also involves system design, resource linkage, and risk recognition.
Common confusion 3: believing EMS has value only during emergencies. Chapter 3 challenges that idea.

reviews.summaryZh: 这章讲法律责任、过失、病患关系、documentation 和 reportable cases，帮助建立 legal boundary。它真正回答的是：你不只是要把事做完，还要在法律上站得住。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter covers legal accountability, negligence, the patient relationship, documentation, and reportable cases to establish legal boundaries. At its core, it asks not only whether the job gets done, but whether it stands up legally.
highlightsZh: 搞清 negligence 和 accountability
知道 documentation 的法律价值
理解现场责任与 reportable cases
highlightsEn: Clarify negligence and accountability
Know the legal value of documentation
Understand scene responsibilities and reportable cases
keyPointsZh: 法律问题不是后置思考，而是 clinical action 从一开始就附带的边界。
paramedic–patient relationship 一旦建立，就意味着 duty、responsibility 和 accountability。
negligence 不是“我不是故意的”就没问题，而是要看是否达到合理专业标准。
documentation 不只是记录你做了什么，也是法律上说明你为什么这么做的重要证据。
crime scene responsibility 和 reportable cases 说明 EMS 不只是医学角色，也承担法定责任。
licensure / certification / regulation 不是行政细节，而是执业合法性的基础。
keyPointsEn: Legal issues are not an afterthought; they shape the boundaries of clinical action from the very beginning.
Once the paramedic–patient relationship exists, duty, responsibility, and accountability follow.
Negligence is not avoided just because harm was unintentional; the question is whether the standard of care was met.
Documentation is not only a record of what you did, but also evidence of why you did it.
Crime scene responsibility and reportable cases show that EMS has legal duties beyond pure clinical care.
Licensure, certification, and regulation are not administrative trivia—they are the foundation of lawful practice.
mustKnowZh: 要把 documentation 当成法律工具，而不只是工作日志。
要理解 negligence 和 accountability 常常和你有没有按标准做、有没有清楚记录有关。
要知道 EMS 现场行为可能同时产生医疗后果和法律后果。
mustKnowEn: Treat documentation as a legal tool, not just a work log.
Understand that negligence and accountability often relate to whether you met standards and documented clearly.
Know that EMS scene actions may create both medical and legal consequences.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：觉得只要出发点是好的，法律上就没问题。实际上标准、过程和记录同样重要。
常见误区 2：把 documentation 当成最后补一下的文书工作。其实它常是你最重要的防线之一。
常见误区 3：以为 legal issue 只在上法庭时才 relevant。其实它从你接触病人的第一刻就已经开始。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: thinking that good intentions automatically protect you legally. In reality, standards, process, and records matter too.
Common confusion 2: treating documentation as something to fill in at the end. In reality, it is often one of your strongest protections.
Common confusion 3: believing legal issues matter only in court. In reality, they begin the moment you make patient contact.

reviews.summaryZh: 这章处理 clinical grey zone，重点在 patient autonomy、resuscitation 决策、integrity 和专业伦理框架。它真正训练的是：不是每个难题都能用法律条文直接解决，你还需要价值判断。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter deals with clinical gray zones, focusing on patient autonomy, resuscitation decisions, integrity, and professional ethical frameworks. Its deeper lesson is that not every hard problem can be solved by a legal rule alone—you also need value-based judgment.
highlightsZh: 学会区分 legal vs ethical
理解 patient autonomy
建立灰区判断框架
highlightsEn: Differentiate legal vs ethical issues
Understand patient autonomy
Build a framework for gray-zone decisions
keyPointsZh: ethics 不是跟法律重复，而是在法律之外处理“应不应该”的问题。
patient autonomy 是院前急救中的核心伦理原则之一。
withholding / withdrawing resuscitation 不只是 protocol 问题，也牵涉价值判断和专业边界。
professional integrity 不是抽象道德感，而是日常 decision-making 的一部分。
有些 clinical situation 没有完美答案，所以要靠 ethical framework 做更稳的判断。
keyPointsEn: Ethics does not simply repeat law; it addresses whether something should be done, not just whether it can be done.
Patient autonomy is one of the central ethical principles in prehospital care.
Withholding or withdrawing resuscitation is not only a protocol issue, but also a matter of values and professional boundaries.
Professional integrity is not abstract morality; it is part of everyday clinical decision-making.
Some clinical situations have no perfect answer, so an ethical framework helps produce more stable judgment.
mustKnowZh: 要学会分开 legal answer 和 ethical answer，它们可能重叠，但不总是同一个东西。
要理解 autonomy 和 beneficence 常常会互相拉扯。
要知道伦理题不是背标准答案，而是背判断框架。
mustKnowEn: Learn to separate the legal answer from the ethical answer—they may overlap, but they are not always the same thing.
Understand that autonomy and beneficence can pull in different directions.
Know that ethics is not about memorizing perfect answers, but about learning a judgment framework.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：觉得 legal = ethical。实际上合法不一定等于最合适，反之亦然。
常见误区 2：觉得伦理题太主观，所以不用认真学。其实它在高风险灰区里非常关键。
常见误区 3：认为 patient autonomy 就等于病人说什么都照做。实际上 autonomy 也要放在 capacity、context 和专业责任里理解。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: assuming legal = ethical. In reality, what is lawful is not always what is most appropriate, and vice versa.
Common confusion 2: dismissing ethics as too subjective to study seriously. In fact, it matters most in high-risk gray zones.

Common confusion 3: assuming patient autonomy means simply doing whatever the patient says. In reality, autonomy must be understood in the context of capacity, circumstances, and professional responsibility.

reviews.summaryZh: 这章是后面所有 disease process 的底层科学，核心在于解释病人为什么会变成现在这样。它不是让你背生化，而是让你能把 shock、inflammation、acid-base imbalance、cell injury 和临床表现连起来。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter is the scientific foundation for later disease processes. Its purpose is not to make you memorize biochemistry, but to help you connect shock, inflammation, acid-base imbalance, cellular injury, and the patient’s clinical presentation.
highlightsZh: 理解 cell / tissue level 的病理变化
把 acid-base、hypoperfusion、inflammation 和 disease process 连起来
为后面所有 medical emergency chapters 打底
highlightsEn: Understand pathology at the cell and tissue level
Link acid-base balance, hypoperfusion, inflammation, and disease processes
Build the foundation for all later medical-emergency chapters
keyPointsZh: Pathophysiology 的核心不是名词，而是机制：病人为什么会恶化、为什么会代偿、为什么会失代偿。
低灌注（hypoperfusion）是后面很多急症的共同主线，不只是 shock 章节才会用到。
acid-base balance 会影响细胞功能、器官表现和病人整体稳定度。
cell injury 和 inflammation 是许多疾病过程的共同语言。
如果你不理解底层 pathophysiology，后面的症状、体征和 treatment 会变成死记硬背。
keyPointsEn: The core of pathophysiology is not terminology but mechanism: why the patient worsens, why they compensate, and why they decompensate.
Hypoperfusion is a shared pathway across many emergencies, not just the shock chapter.
Acid-base balance affects cellular function, organ behavior, and overall patient stability.
Cell injury and inflammation are common languages across many disease processes.
Without pathophysiology, later signs, symptoms, and treatments become memorization instead of understanding.
mustKnowZh: 要把“症状”往回追到机制，而不是只背表面表现。
要知道很多 emergency 最终都会回到 perfusion、oxygen delivery、cell injury 这些底层问题。
要把这章当成整个 medical 部分的“解释器”。
mustKnowEn: Trace symptoms back to mechanism rather than memorizing surface presentation only.
Know that many emergencies ultimately return to perfusion, oxygen delivery, and cellular injury.
Treat this chapter as the “interpreter” for the entire medical section.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：把 pathophysiology 当成只为了考试的理论章。实际上它决定你后面对病人的理解深度。
常见误区 2：觉得 acid-base、inflammation 这些太抽象。其实它们会直接体现在呼吸、循环和意识变化里。
常见误区 3：把 shock 只理解成血压低。实际上更早期、更底层的问题是 perfusion failure。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: treating pathophysiology as a theory chapter only for exams. In reality, it determines how deeply you understand patients later.
Common confusion 2: thinking acid-base balance and inflammation are too abstract. In reality, they show up directly in breathing, circulation, and mental-status changes.
Common confusion 3: reducing shock to low blood pressure. The earlier and deeper issue is perfusion failure.

reviews.summaryZh: 药理学研究的是药物如何与人体发生作用，以及这些作用如何被安全地用于改善病人结局。在 EMS 里，药理学绝不只是背药名，它真正讲的是：药为什么有效、什么时候可能危险、给药途径和剂量如何影响作用，以及为什么同一种药在不同病人身上会出现不同反应。这一章是后面几乎所有用药决策的基础。
reviews.summaryEn: Pharmacology is the study of how medications interact with the human body and how those interactions can be used safely to improve patient outcomes. In EMS, pharmacology is not just about memorizing drug names. It is about understanding why a medication works, when it may be dangerous, how route and dose affect its action, and why the same medication can produce different effects in different patients. This chapter is foundational because nearly every later medication decision depends on it.
highlightsZh: 药理学的核心是机制和安全，不只是记忆。
药代动力学和药效学解释的是药物作用的不同部分。
剂量、途径、浓度、副作用和相互作用都很重要。
一种药即使适应证对，也可能在错误病人或错误场景里变得危险。
真正的 medication thinking，本质上是 patient-specific physiology thinking。
highlightsEn: Pharmacology is about mechanism and safety, not just memorization.
Pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics explain different parts of drug action.
Dose, route, concentration, side effects, and interactions all matter.
A medication can be indicated and still be dangerous in the wrong patient or the wrong context.
Medication thinking is really patient-specific physiology thinking.
keyPointsZh: 药代动力学讲的是机体如何处理药物：吸收、分布、代谢、排泄。药效学讲的是药物如何作用于机体：受体作用、期望效果和不良效果。真正理解药物行为，这两部分都不能少。
给药途径很重要，因为它决定药物多快、多稳定地到达作用部位。同一种药经静脉、口服、吸入或肌注，表现可能完全不同。
剂量很重要，因为“太少没效果”和“太多出危险”之间，往往就是临床上真正需要把握的界线。很多 medication safety 问题，本质上就是 dose safety 问题。
副作用、禁忌证和药物相互作用绝不是边角信息，它们常常决定了一种本来正确的药，在某个具体病人身上变成不安全的药。
药理学永远和病人本身有关。年龄、体型、慢病、器官功能、脱水状态和合并用药，都会改变药物表现。
好的药理思维不只是问“这个问题该用什么药”，还会问“这药在这个病人身上现在会带来什么风险”。
keyPointsEn: Pharmacokinetics explains what the body does to the drug: absorption, distribution, metabolism, and elimination. Pharmacodynamics explains what the drug does to the body: receptor effects, desired actions, and unwanted effects. You need both to understand real medication behavior.

Route matters because it changes how quickly and how reliably the medication reaches its target. A drug given intravenously behaves differently from the same drug given orally, inhaled, or intramuscularly.

Dose matters because the difference between too little effect and too much effect may be clinically significant or dangerous. Medication safety is often a dose-safety problem as much as a drug-choice problem.
Side effects, contraindications, and interactions are not secondary details. They are often the reason a correct medication becomes an unsafe medication in a specific patient.
Pharmacology is always patient-dependent. Age, body size, chronic disease, organ function, hydration status, and concurrent medications can all change how a drug behaves.
Good pharmacology thinking means asking not just “What drug fits this problem?” but also “What risk does this drug create in this patient right now?”
mustKnowZh: 你必须清楚区分药代动力学和药效学。
药物安全取决于病人，不只取决于药名。
剂量、途径、副作用和相互作用属于核心知识，不是附加知识。
一种药有适应证，并不等于它自动安全。
mustKnowEn: You must clearly distinguish pharmacokinetics from pharmacodynamics.
Medication safety depends on the patient, not just on the drug label.
Dose, route, side effects, and interactions are core knowledge, not extra knowledge.
A drug being indicated does not make it automatically safe.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：药理学就是背清单。实际上它是在理解机制和风险。
常见误区 2：药物和 complaint 对上就一定合适。实际上病人背景可能让同一种药变危险。
常见误区 3：副作用只是小细节。实际上副作用常常决定这药的安全边界。
常见误区 4：用药选择和病人 assessment 是分开的。实际上 assessment 质量会直接决定 medication decision 质量。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: pharmacology is memorizing lists. In reality, it is understanding mechanism and risk.
Common confusion 2: if the medication matches the complaint, it must be appropriate. In reality, patient context may make the same medication dangerous.
Common confusion 3: side effects are minor details. In reality, side effects often define the safety boundary of the drug.
Common confusion 4: medication choice is separate from patient assessment. In reality, medication choice depends on assessment quality.

reviews.titleZh: Chapter 8 — Vascular Access and Medication Administration
reviews.titleEn: Chapter 8 — Vascular Access and Medication Administration
reviews.summaryZh: 血管通路与给药管理，是把药理学真正变成临床动作的章节。这一章教你如何建立安全通路，液体和药物如何进入体内，如何准确计算剂量和速率，以及为什么给药是一个 patient-safety process，而不是机械技术动作。它是最偏执行、也最容易一错就出事的章节之一。
reviews.summaryEn: Vascular access and medication administration turn pharmacology into action. This chapter teaches how to establish safe access, how fluids and medications enter the body, how to calculate doses and rates accurately, and how to think about administration as a patient-safety process rather than a technical ritual. It is one of the most operationally important chapters because mistakes here are immediate, concrete, and potentially dangerous.
highlightsZh: 通路只有在安全、通畅、适当时才有价值。
剂量和速率计算属于 patient-safety skill。
给药途径和浓度会改变药物表现。
无菌原则和污染物处理属于治疗安全的一部分。
把药打进病人体内，不如把药正确地打进去更重要。
highlightsEn: Access is useful only when it is safe, functional, and appropriate.
Dose and rate calculation are patient-safety skills.
Route and concentration change how medication behaves.
Asepsis and disposal practices are part of treatment safety.
Getting medication into the patient is never more important than getting it in correctly.
keyPointsZh: 建立血管通路不只是一个操作动作，而是在为液体、药物，甚至必要时的实验室信息建立一条可靠治疗路径。如果通路位置不对、维持不好或适应证判断错误，整个干预都会变得不稳定。
液体和电解质之所以重要，是因为 EMS 常在处理灌注失败、休克、脱水、药物输送和整体生理不稳定，而不是单纯“挂点液”。
给药包含的不只是 route，还包括剂量、浓度、速率、时机、适应证和监测。少考虑一个环节，就会多一层风险。
剂量计算错误属于高风险错误。尤其在儿科、高浓度药物和持续输注药物里，一个小数学错误就可能变成大临床错误。
无菌技术重要，是因为治疗本身也可能制造新的伤害；忽视感染风险、污染或不安全处置，等于把并发风险带进照护过程。
一旦开始给药或补液，就必须继续 reassess。通路可能失效、可能渗漏、药物效果可能与预期不同，而病人状态也可能快速变化。
keyPointsEn: Vascular access is not just a procedure. It is the creation of a reliable treatment path for fluids, medications, and sometimes laboratory information. If the line is poorly placed, poorly maintained, or poorly chosen, the whole intervention becomes unstable.
Fluids and electrolytes matter because EMS is often treating perfusion failure, shock states, dehydration, medication delivery, and physiologic instability—not simply “hanging fluid.”
Medication administration includes route, dose, concentration, rate, timing, indication, and monitoring. Leaving out any of these creates risk.
Dose calculation errors are high-risk errors. Small math mistakes can become large clinical mistakes, especially in pediatrics, concentrated drugs, and infusion-based medications.
Aseptic technique matters because treatment can create new harm if infection risk, contamination, or unsafe disposal is ignored.
A line or medication should always be reassessed after administration begins. Access may fail, infiltration may occur, the drug effect may differ from expected, and the patient may change quickly.
mustKnowZh: 剂量和速率计算是安全关键任务。
给药途径、浓度和适应证必须一起考虑。
建立通路不是终点，后续 reassessment 是硬要求。
只有技术动作、没有安全思维，属于不完整 practice。
mustKnowEn: Dose and rate calculation are safety-critical tasks.
Route, concentration, and indication must always be considered together.
Access establishment is not the end point; reassessment is mandatory.
Procedure skill without safety thinking is incomplete practice.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：通路建立好了，任务就结束了。实际上通路功能和病人反应仍然必须继续监测。
常见误区 2：给药主要是技术问题。实际上它同时是 reasoning、calculation 和 safety 问题。
常见误区 3：液体永远是中性支持。实际上补液在不同生理状态下可能有帮助、可能有害，也可能根本不适合。
常见误区 4：数学错误只是小错误。实际上剂量错误很容易直接变成严重 patient harm。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: once vascular access is obtained, the job is finished. In reality, line function and patient response still need monitoring.
Common confusion 2: medication administration is mainly technical. In reality, it is a reasoning, calculation, and safety process.
Common confusion 3: fluids are neutral support. In reality, fluids can help, harm, or be inappropriate depending on the patient’s physiology.
Common confusion 4: math errors are minor. In reality, dose errors can become major patient harm.

reviews.summaryZh: 这章讲不同年龄层的人类发展特点，重点不是背年龄定义，而是理解不同阶段在解剖、生理、心理和沟通上的差异。它真正回答的是：为什么不同年龄的病人，不能用同一套 assessment 逻辑硬套。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter covers human development across age groups. The goal is not to memorize age labels, but to understand how anatomy, physiology, psychology, and communication differ by stage. At its core, it explains why patients of different ages cannot be forced into the same assessment logic.
highlightsZh: 理解不同年龄层不是同一种病人的缩放版
把发展阶段和沟通、评估、风险判断连起来
为 pediatrics、geriatrics 和 family interaction 打底
highlightsEn: Understand that age groups are not scaled versions of the same patient
Link developmental stage to communication, assessment, and risk judgment
Build the foundation for pediatrics, geriatrics, and family interaction
keyPointsZh: 婴儿、幼儿、学龄儿童、青少年、成人和老年人，在正常表现、风险和互动方式上都不同。
发展阶段会影响病史采集方式、合作程度、对疼痛和恐惧的表达、以及 family involvement 的重要性。
年龄相关差异不只是心理问题，也包括解剖和生理层面的真实差异。
“正常”生命体征、行为和反应方式，会随着年龄变化。
理解 human development 可以减少误判，例如把正常年龄表现误认成异常，或把危险表现误认成“只是小孩这样”。
keyPointsEn: Infants, toddlers, school-age children, adolescents, adults, and older adults differ in normal presentation, risk, and interaction style.
Developmental stage affects how history is gathered, how much cooperation is possible, how pain and fear are expressed, and how important family involvement may be.
Age-related differences are not only psychological; they also involve real anatomic and physiologic differences.
“Normal” vital signs, behavior, and response patterns change with age.
Understanding human development reduces misinterpretation—for example, mistaking age-appropriate behavior for pathology or missing dangerous findings by thinking “that’s just how kids are.”
mustKnowZh: 要把年龄层差异看成 assessment 基础，不是附加知识。
要知道 developmental stage 会直接影响 communication strategy。
后面的 pediatrics 和 geriatrics，很多判断都建立在这一章。
mustKnowEn: Treat age-related differences as part of the core assessment foundation, not as extra trivia.
Know that developmental stage directly affects communication strategy.
Much of the reasoning in later pediatrics and geriatrics is built on this chapter.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：把儿童当缩小版成人。实际上 anatomy、physiology 和 behavior 都不同。
常见误区 2：把老年人只当“年纪大一点的成人”。实际上他们的 baseline、reserve 和 presentation 常很不一样。
常见误区 3：觉得 human development 只是理论内容。其实它会直接影响你现场如何问、如何看、如何判断。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: treating children as small adults. In reality, anatomy, physiology, and behavior all differ.
Common confusion 2: treating older adults as just older versions of younger adults. In reality, baseline, reserve, and presentation are often quite different.
Common confusion 3: thinking human development is only theory. In reality, it directly changes how you ask, observe, and judge in the field.

reviews.summaryZh: 这章重点在 patient communication：如何建立 rapport、怎么问问题、怎么看非语言讯号、以及在特殊场景下如何减少沟通失败。它真正训练的是：你不只是会说话，而是会通过沟通拿到临床上真正有用的信息。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter focuses on patient communication: how to build rapport, ask questions effectively, interpret nonverbal cues, and reduce communication failure in special situations. At its core, it trains you not just to speak, but to use communication to obtain clinically useful information.
highlightsZh: 把 communication 当成 assessment 工具
理解开放式问题和积极倾听的价值
注意 nonverbal cues 与情绪状态
highlightsEn: Treat communication as an assessment tool
Understand the value of open-ended questions and active listening
Pay attention to nonverbal cues and emotional state
keyPointsZh: 沟通不只是礼貌，而是 clinical assessment 的一部分。
开放式问题往往比封闭式问题更容易获得完整病史。
病人怎么说、什么时候停顿、有没有回避、是否紧张，本身都可能是临床线索。
非语言讯号（表情、姿势、语气、眼神、互动方式）常和病情、情绪、信任度相关。
特殊病人、敏感话题、高压力现场，都要求你调整沟通方式，而不是只重复同一句话。
keyPointsEn: Communication is not just politeness; it is part of clinical assessment.
Open-ended questions often obtain a fuller history than purely closed questions.
How the patient speaks, hesitates, avoids, or appears anxious may itself be clinically relevant.
Nonverbal cues—facial expression, posture, tone, eye contact, interaction style—often relate to illness, emotion, and trust.
Special patients, sensitive topics, and high-stress scenes require adjustment in communication style rather than simply repeating the same words.
mustKnowZh: 要把 rapport 建立看成 assessment 的第一步之一。
要记住 active listening 不是沉默，而是有方向地接信息。
要知道沟通方式会影响病人愿不愿意说、能不能说、以及你能不能得到真信息。
mustKnowEn: Building rapport should be seen as one of the first steps in assessment.
Remember that active listening is not passive silence; it is directed information gathering.
Know that communication style affects whether the patient is willing or able to share accurate information.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：觉得 communication 是软技能，和 clinical quality 分开。实际上问法会直接影响 diagnosis direction。
常见误区 2：以为说得越快越专业。实际上病人理解不了，就拿不到有效信息。
常见误区 3：只关注病人说出来的话，不看 nonverbal cues。这样会漏掉很多重要线索。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: treating communication as a soft skill separate from clinical quality. In reality, the way you ask questions shapes diagnostic direction.
Common confusion 2: thinking faster speech sounds more professional. In reality, if the patient cannot understand, useful information is lost.
Common confusion 3: focusing only on spoken words and ignoring nonverbal cues. That misses many important clues.

reviews.summaryZh: 这章讲 research and quality，目标不是把你训练成学者，而是让你形成 evidence-based EMS 思维，知道系统如何衡量质量、回顾错误、改进流程。它回答的是：为什么“经验”还不够，你还要会看证据和系统表现。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter addresses research and quality. Its goal is not to turn you into an academic researcher, but to build an evidence-based EMS mindset and show how systems measure quality, review error, and improve processes. It answers why “experience” alone is not enough—you also need evidence and system awareness.
highlightsZh: 建立 evidence-based EMS 思维
理解 quality improvement 和 patient safety 的关系
知道研究设计与证据质量会影响实践
highlightsEn: Build an evidence-based EMS mindset
Understand the relationship between quality improvement and patient safety
Know that study design and evidence quality influence practice
keyPointsZh: 研究与质量管理的目标不是抽象学术，而是改善 patient outcomes 和 system performance。
evidence-based practice 强调把 best evidence、clinical judgment 和 patient context 放在一起。
quality improvement 不只是找人犯错，而是识别系统问题、流程问题和可改进点。
incident reporting、benchmarking、quality indicators 等概念，都是 system learning 的工具。
patient safety culture 的重点在于透明、学习和持续改进，而不是单纯 blame。
keyPointsEn: The purpose of research and quality work is not abstract scholarship, but improvement of patient outcomes and system performance.
Evidence-based practice combines the best evidence with clinical judgment and patient context.
Quality improvement is not just about finding blame; it is about identifying system problems, process problems, and areas for improvement.
Incident reporting, benchmarking, and quality indicators are tools for system learning.
A patient-safety culture emphasizes transparency, learning, and continuous improvement rather than simple blame.
mustKnowZh: 要知道 evidence-based practice 不是“只听论文”，而是 evidence + judgment + context。
要把 QI 看成 frontline provider 也参与的工作。
要明白 patient safety 是系统结果，不只是个人表现。
mustKnowEn: Know that evidence-based practice is not “just follow the literature,” but evidence + judgment + context.
Treat quality improvement as something frontline providers participate in, not only managers.
Understand that patient safety is a systems outcome, not just an individual trait.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：把研究章节当成与现场无关。实际上它决定你为什么做现在做的事。
常见误区 2：把 QI 误解成追责工具。其实它更应该是学习工具。
常见误区 3：觉得经验比证据更高级。实际上经验如果不被验证，可能只是重复偏差。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: treating the research chapter as unrelated to field work. In reality, it explains why current practice exists.
Common confusion 2: misunderstanding quality improvement as a punishment tool. It should be a learning tool.
Common confusion 3: assuming experience is automatically superior to evidence. Experience without validation may simply repeat bias.

reviews.titleZh: Chapter 12 — Expanded EMS Systems and Community Paramedicine in Canada
reviews.titleEn: Chapter 12 — Expanded EMS Systems and Community Paramedicine in Canada
reviews.summaryZh: 这章把 EMS 的视角从传统 response-and-transport 拉向 expanded systems 和 community paramedicine，讨论角色扩展、nontransport 和未来系统方向。它真正要你看到的是：EMS 不只是救护车，它也是健康系统的一部分。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter shifts the EMS perspective from traditional response-and-transport toward expanded systems and community paramedicine, covering role expansion, nontransport, and future system direction. Its deeper lesson is that EMS is not just an ambulance service—it is part of the wider health system.
highlightsZh: 认识 expanded EMS role
理解 community paramedicine 的价值
知道 nontransport 涉及 clinical、system 和 risk 三层判断
highlightsEn: Recognize the expanded EMS role
Understand the value of community paramedicine
Know that nontransport involves clinical, system, and risk-based judgment
keyPointsZh: 传统 EMS 模式强调 response 和 transport，但系统正在向更广泛的健康角色扩展。
community paramedicine 强调 prevention、follow-up、resource connection 和 chronic-care support。
expanded EMS systems 要求 provider 不只是会处理急性问题，也要理解系统 flow 和资源整合。
nontransport 不是简单地“不送”，而是一个和适应证、风险、follow-up 能力密切相关的决策。
这章会改变你对 EMS identity 的理解：从 emergency responder 扩展到 broader health-system participant。
keyPointsEn: Traditional EMS models emphasize response and transport, but the system is evolving toward broader health roles.
Community paramedicine emphasizes prevention, follow-up, resource connection, and chronic-care support.
Expanded EMS systems require providers to understand not just acute treatment, but also system flow and resource integration.
Nontransport is not simply “not taking the patient”; it is a decision closely tied to indication, risk, and follow-up capacity.
This chapter changes EMS identity from emergency responder alone to broader health-system participant.
mustKnowZh: 要知道 role expansion 不是“越做越多”而已，而是系统定位的改变。
要理解 nontransport 不是图省事，而是高要求 decision-making。
要把 community paramedicine 看成 EMS 和公共健康、primary care 的连接点。
mustKnowEn: Know that role expansion is not just “doing more,” but a shift in system position.
Understand that nontransport is not about convenience; it is high-level decision-making.
Treat community paramedicine as a bridge between EMS, public health, and primary care.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：觉得 EMS 只要会急救就够了。Chapter 12 在打破这种狭义理解。
常见误区 2：把 nontransport 理解成不负责。实际上它可能比 transport 更需要严密判断。
常见误区 3：觉得 community paramedicine 只是附加项目。其实它反映的是系统方向变化。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: thinking EMS only needs acute emergency skills. Chapter 12 challenges that narrow view.
Common confusion 2: treating nontransport as careless or lazy. In reality, it may require more disciplined judgment than transport.
Common confusion 3: seeing community paramedicine as a side project. In reality, it reflects a larger systems-level shift.

reviews.summaryZh: 气道管理与通气是急救里最优先的生命支持问题，因为如果病人不能维持通畅气道和有效气体交换，就不可能长期存活。这一章教你的不是单纯背设备，而是学会尽早识别气道受损，分清气道问题、氧合问题和通气问题，并判断什么时候基础支持够用、什么时候必须立刻升级处理。核心目标是在病人彻底呼吸衰竭前就及时介入。
reviews.summaryEn: Airway management and ventilation are the first life-support priorities in emergency care because a patient cannot survive for long without a patent airway and effective gas exchange. This chapter teaches you how to recognize airway compromise early, distinguish airway problems from oxygenation and ventilation problems, and decide when basic support is enough versus when immediate escalation is required. The goal is not simply to memorize devices, but to understand how breathing fails, how patients compensate, and how to intervene before complete respiratory collapse occurs.
highlightsZh: 病人即使还在呼吸、还能说话，也可能正在走向呼吸衰竭。
气道、氧合、通气彼此相关，但不是同一个概念。
呼吸工作量、疲劳、意识状态和气道保护能力，往往比单个监测数字更早暴露危险。
吸引、体位、气道辅助器、给氧和辅助通气分别是在解决不同层级的问题。
真正的能力不是会摆设备，而是能及早看出失败并按正确顺序升级支持。
highlightsEn: A patient may still be breathing and talking while moving toward respiratory failure.
Airway, oxygenation, and ventilation are related but not identical concepts.
Work of breathing, fatigue, mental status, and airway protection often reveal danger earlier than a single monitor number.
Suction, positioning, airway adjuncts, oxygen, and assisted ventilation each solve different problems.
The real skill is recognizing failure early and escalating support in the right order.
keyPointsZh: Airway 指的是空气能不能实际进出呼吸道；oxygenation 指氧气能不能进入血液；ventilation 指病人能不能把空气有效地进出肺部、完成氧气与二氧化碳交换。这三者相关，但一个出问题不代表另外两个一定同样严重。
气道受损可能来自软组织阻塞、舌后坠、血液、呕吐物、分泌物、水肿、创伤或异物。意识下降之所以危险，是因为病人可能在完全堵塞前，就已经先失去保护气道的能力。
气道受损的常见线索包括鼾声、痰鸣音、喉鸣、可见阻塞、无法处理分泌物、血液或呕吐物，以及明显的气道保护能力下降。这些线索不只是告诉你“有问题”，还提示你更可能需要哪一类处理。
病人即使气道通，也可能氧合失败，因为肺部本身无法有效交换气体。反过来，病人也可能暂时还有氧合，但其实通气已经不行，尤其当潮气量不足、疲劳上升、二氧化碳排不出去时。
呼吸工作量是呼吸评估里价值最高的部分之一。要看辅助呼吸肌使用、体位改变、浅快呼吸、说话断句、鼻翼扇动、三凹征、明显缺气感和疲劳表现。很多时候，这些危险线索会早于 pulse oximetry 的明显下降出现。
疲劳是一个重要转折点。病人如果从一开始非常用力呼吸，后来突然显得“安静一点”，不一定是在好转，也可能是在逐渐失去维持通气的能力。所以重复 reassessment 非常关键。
Suction 绝不是小技能。在很多 airway emergency 里，吸引正是后续所有气道处理得以进行的前提，因为它能清掉阻碍视野和气流的血液、呕吐物和分泌物。
Airway adjuncts 是支撑工具，不是万能解法。它们的作用是在病人自己不能稳定维持气道时帮忙撑住，但并不能取代临床判断，也不能解决所有呼吸问题。
Oxygen therapy 的目标是改善氧输送，但单纯给氧并不能修复通气不足。如果病人本身空气进出得很差，即使已经在给氧，也可能仍然需要辅助通气。
辅助通气应该在病人已经不能维持有效通气时就开始考虑，而不是一定要等到完全停呼吸才接手。拖延是气道处理中最经典的错误之一。
高级气道管理是升级步骤，不是所有气道处理的起点。好的 airway management 总是先从评估、体位、吸引、基础手法、给氧支持，以及判断病人是在代偿还是在走向失败开始。
所有气道干预最终都服务于同一个目标：保持气道通畅、维持氧合、支持通气，并阻止病人进入完全呼吸衰竭。
keyPointsEn: Airway refers to whether air can physically move into and out of the respiratory tract. Oxygenation refers to whether oxygen reaches the blood. Ventilation refers to whether the patient is moving air effectively enough to exchange oxygen and carbon dioxide. A patient can have one of these problems without having all three at the same degree.

Airway compromise may come from soft-tissue obstruction, the tongue falling back, blood, vomit, secretions, swelling, trauma, or foreign bodies. A reduced level of consciousness is dangerous because the patient may lose the ability to protect the airway even before complete obstruction occurs.

Signs of airway compromise include snoring, gurgling, stridor, visible obstruction, poor airway protection, pooling secretions, and an inability to handle blood or vomit. These clues matter because they tell you not only that something is wrong, but also what type of intervention is most likely to help.

A patient may have a patent airway but still fail oxygenation because the lungs are not exchanging gas effectively. Likewise, a patient may be oxygenating temporarily but ventilating poorly, especially if tidal volume is low, fatigue is rising, or carbon dioxide clearance is failing.

Work of breathing is one of the highest-value parts of respiratory assessment. Look for accessory muscle use, posture changes, shallow breathing, inability to speak in full sentences, nasal flaring, retractions, air hunger, and visible exhaustion. These signs often warn you before pulse oximetry shows obvious collapse.

Fatigue is a major turning point. A patient who looks slightly calmer after extreme respiratory effort may not be improving; the patient may be tiring out and losing the ability to maintain ventilation. This is why repeated reassessment is essential.
Suction is not a minor skill. In many airway emergencies, suction is what makes the rest of airway care possible by removing blood, vomit, and secretions that block visualization and airflow.

Airway adjuncts are support tools, not magical fixes. Their job is to help maintain patency when the patient cannot do it reliably. They do not replace judgment, and they do not solve every respiratory problem.

Oxygen therapy is used to improve oxygen delivery, but oxygen alone does not fix inadequate ventilation. If the patient is moving air poorly, assisted ventilation may be required even if oxygen is already being delivered.
Assisted ventilation should be considered when the patient is no longer maintaining effective ventilation, not only when breathing has fully stopped. Waiting too long is a classic airway error.

Advanced airway management is an escalation step, not the starting point of all airway care. Good airway management always begins with assessment, positioning, suction, basic maneuvers, oxygen support, and recognition of whether the patient is still compensating or beginning to fail.
The purpose of every airway intervention is the same: preserve airway patency, maintain oxygenation, support ventilation, and prevent the patient from progressing to complete respiratory failure.
mustKnowZh: 你必须清楚分开气道问题、氧合问题和通气问题。混在一起想，处理就很容易错。
不要因为病人还能说话就低估风险。会说话只是线索，不是稳定保证。
呼吸工作量、疲劳和意识状态，很多时候和监测数字一样重要，甚至更早提示危险。
辅助通气不只是给完全停呼吸的病人，它也适用于已经不能有效通气的病人。
气道管理是一条逐级升级的支持链，而不是设备清单。
mustKnowEn: You must clearly separate airway problems from oxygenation and ventilation problems. If you confuse them, you will choose the wrong intervention.
Do not let a speaking patient fool you into underestimating risk. Speech is a clue, not a guarantee of stability.
Work of breathing, fatigue, and mental status often matter as much as monitor readings.
Assisted ventilation is not only for apnea. It is for failure of effective ventilation.
Airway management is an escalating sequence of support, not a device checklist.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：病人还能说话，就说明气道没事。实际上病人可能已经在疲劳、在肿胀、在出血，或者已经快失去气道保护能力。
常见误区 2：给氧就等于处理了呼吸窘迫。给氧也许能改善氧合，但并不会自动修复通气不足。
常见误区 3：pulse oximeter 看起来还行，病人就安全。实际上病人仍可能正在疲劳、通气变差，或即将快速恶化。
常见误区 4：气道管理就是高级气道。事实上，大多数 airway management 都是从识别、体位、吸引和基础支持开始的。
常见误区 5：辅助通气要等到完全停呼吸才上。实际上等到那时，往往已经错过了更安全的介入窗口。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: If the patient is talking, the airway is fine. In reality, a patient may still be tiring, swelling, bleeding, or losing airway protection.
Common confusion 2: Oxygen fixes respiratory distress. Oxygen may improve oxygenation, but it does not automatically fix poor ventilation.
Common confusion 3: If the pulse oximeter looks okay, the patient is safe. A patient may still be tiring out, ventilating poorly, or heading toward rapid deterioration.
Common confusion 4: Airway management means advanced airway placement. In reality, most airway management begins with recognition, positioning, suction, and basic support.
Common confusion 5: Assisted ventilation should wait until full respiratory arrest. In reality, waiting that long may mean you have already missed the safest intervention window.

reviews.summaryZh: 机械通气是在基础气道和通气支持已经不够时，继续往上升级的生命支持方式。这一章教你的不是单纯“呼吸机怎么开”，而是病人什么时候已经需要更高级的通气支持、为什么监测和病人反应跟机器本身一样重要，以及为什么通气管理是一种持续主动管理，而不是设好参数就结束。
reviews.summaryEn: Mechanical ventilation is the continuation of airway and ventilation support when basic methods are no longer enough. This chapter teaches how assisted and mechanical ventilation support patients who cannot maintain adequate gas exchange on their own, why monitoring matters as much as the device itself, and how to think about ventilation as an active management process rather than a passive machine setting.
highlightsZh: 机械通气是高级呼吸支持，不只是机器操作技巧。
病人的生理状态永远比单独的机器参数更重要。
监测、重复评估和调整是通气管理本身的一部分。
机械通气的存在，是为了在病人无法独立完成有效气体交换时接手支持。
这一章是第13章的延伸，不是跟第13章分开的平行章节。
highlightsEn: Mechanical ventilation is advanced respiratory support, not just a machine skill.
The patient’s physiology always matters more than the machine settings alone.
Monitoring, reassessment, and adjustment are essential parts of ventilation management.
Mechanical ventilation exists to maintain effective gas exchange when the patient can no longer do so independently.
This chapter builds on Chapter 13, not separate from it.
keyPointsZh: 机械通气要理解成在病人已经无法维持足够通气时，对呼吸支持的升级。
机器只是其中一部分，真正的 ventilator management 还要同时看病人的反应、氧合、胸廓起伏、舒适度、疲劳程度，以及有没有在好转或恶化。
病人需要辅助/机械通气，常见原因包括潮气量不足、疲劳、意识状态下降、进行性呼吸衰竭，或即使基础支持也无法维持气体交换。
机械通气的目标不是让机器参数看起来漂亮，而是维持生命所需的生理状态：氧气进得去、二氧化碳排得出、呼吸工作量下降到不至于崩溃。
不好的通气管理可能导致气体交换进一步变差、病人痛苦增加、支持无效，或延误发现病情恶化。
这一章再次强化 EMS 的核心原则：支持方式必须贴着病人的当前生理状态走，而且病人变了，你的管理也要跟着变。
keyPointsEn: Mechanical ventilation should be understood as an escalation in respiratory support for patients who are failing or unable to maintain adequate ventilation on their own.

The device is only one part of the picture. Ventilator management always requires attention to patient response, oxygenation, chest movement, comfort, fatigue, and evidence of improvement or deterioration.

A patient may require assisted ventilation because of inadequate tidal volume, fatigue, altered mental status, progressive respiratory failure, or inability to sustain gas exchange despite basic support.

The goal of mechanical ventilation is not to make the machine look correct, but to maintain physiology that supports life: oxygen in, carbon dioxide out, and breathing work reduced enough to prevent collapse.
Poor ventilator management may lead to worsening gas exchange, patient distress, ineffective support, or delayed recognition of deterioration.
This chapter reinforces a major EMS principle: support must match the patient’s current physiology, and management must change as the patient changes.
mustKnowZh: 机械通气是持续管理过程，不是一次性设置。
看机器的时候必须同时看病人。
是否需要通气支持，取决于病人生理状态，不是单纯因为设备在手边。
重复评估是硬要求，因为呼吸衰竭本身就是动态变化的。
mustKnowEn: Mechanical ventilation is a management process, not a one-time setup.
The patient must always be assessed together with the machine.
Ventilator support is justified by physiology, not by equipment availability alone.
Reassessment is mandatory because respiratory failure is dynamic.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：机械通气只是高级设备操作。实际上它更像是病人生理管理章节。
常见误区 2：参数设对了就说明病人很好。实际上病人可能仍在疲劳、烦躁、低通气或继续恶化。
常见误区 3：一旦上了高级支持，就不用那么频繁重评。实际上恰恰相反，越高级的支持越需要更密切 reassessment。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: mechanical ventilation is just advanced equipment. In reality, it is a patient-physiology chapter.
Common confusion 2: correct settings mean the patient is doing well. In reality, the patient may still be tiring, agitated, hypoventilating, or worsening.
Common confusion 3: once advanced support is started, reassessment matters less. In reality, reassessment matters more.

reviews.summaryZh: 病史采集是把一个“你还不知道发生了什么的病人”，变成一个有临床意义案例的过程。这一章教你的不是简单问几句，而是如何从病人、家属、旁观者和现场里拿到真正有用的信息，如何通过提问得到可用答案，以及为什么病史不是形式流程，而是诊断和决策的基础之一。
reviews.summaryEn: Patient history is the structured collection of information that turns an unknown patient into a meaningful clinical case. This chapter teaches how to gather the right information from the patient, family, bystanders, and scene; how to ask questions that produce useful answers; and how to recognize that a history is not merely a formality, but one of the foundations of diagnosis and decision-making.
highlightsZh: 好的病史会改变整个 assessment 的方向。
开放式问题通常比僵硬的是非题更容易得到有价值的信息。
病人不是唯一的病史来源。
敏感话题依然重要，只是必须用专业方式处理。
病史本身就是决定后面重点查什么的重要工具。
highlightsEn: A good history changes the direction of assessment.
Open-ended questions often provide more useful information than rigid yes/no questioning.
The patient is not the only source of history.
Sensitive topics still matter and must be handled professionally.
History is one of the main tools for deciding what to examine next.
keyPointsZh: 病史采集不是问越多越好，而是要拿到能澄清病人风险、病程变化和可能临床路径的信息。
主诉只是入口，不是全部。高质量病史还包括起始时间、发展过程、伴随症状、相关既往史、用药和整体背景。
你怎么问很重要。病人只有在觉得安全、被听见、被理解时，才更容易给出真实而有价值的信息。
有用病史不一定都来自病人本人。家属、照护者、目击者和现场环境常常能补上关键空白。
好的病史可以帮助你聚焦查体、缩小鉴别方向，并更快看出现在最重要的问题是什么。
keyPointsEn: History gathering is not about asking every possible question; it is about gathering the information that clarifies the patient’s risk, progression, and likely clinical pathways.
The chief complaint is only the starting point. High-quality history includes timing, progression, associated symptoms, relevant past history, medications, and context.
The way you ask matters. Patients are more likely to give useful, accurate information when they feel safe, heard, and understood.
Not all useful history comes directly from the patient. Family, caregivers, witnesses, and the physical scene may provide essential missing pieces.
Good history helps focus the physical exam, improve differential thinking, and identify what matters most right now.
mustKnowZh: 病史是塑造 assessment 和 decision-making 最强的工具之一。
主诉不等于完整病情。
怎么问，很多时候和问什么一样重要。
mustKnowEn: The history is one of the strongest tools for shaping assessment and decision-making.
The chief complaint is not the same as the whole story.
How you ask is often as important as what you ask.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：病史采集就是照着模板问。实际上它是一个需要灵活调整的临床技能。
常见误区 2：查体总比病史更重要。实际上差的病史会把整个查体带偏。
常见误区 3：敏感话题最好别问。实际上重要话题还是得问，只是要专业、合适地问。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: history taking is a script. In reality, it is a flexible clinical skill.
Common confusion 2: the physical exam is always more important than the history. In reality, a poor history can misdirect the entire exam.
Common confusion 3: sensitive topics should be avoided. In reality, important topics still need to be addressed—professionally and appropriately.

reviews.summaryZh: 体格检查是把观察和接触转换成临床信息的过程。这一章教你的不只是“摸一遍、听一遍”，而是如何通过视、触、听、生命体征和整体印象去收集 findings，如何把这些 findings 组织起来，以及为什么一个 exam 只有在真正帮助你理解病人状态时才有价值。
reviews.summaryEn: The physical examination translates observation and contact into clinical information. This chapter teaches how to collect findings through inspection, palpation, auscultation, vital signs, and general impression; how to organize those findings meaningfully; and why an exam is valuable only when it improves understanding of the patient’s actual condition.
highlightsZh: 整体印象往往是最早也最重要的 findings 之一。
生命体征的价值在趋势，不在单个数字。
体格检查必须和病史、现场一起解读。
做得很全，不等于做得很有用。
查体本身有局限，所以必须放在 context 里看。
highlightsEn: General impression is one of the earliest and most important findings.
Vital signs matter as trends, not isolated numbers.
The physical exam must be connected to the history and scene.
A complete exam is not the same as a useful exam.
The exam has limits and must always be interpreted in context.
keyPointsZh: 体格检查的价值在于产出可解释的信息，而不是把动作做完。每一个 finding 的意义在于它会改变或支持你对病人的整体判断。
整体印象常常会决定整通 call 的节奏。一个看起来 toxic、疲惫、混乱、发绀或明显 distress 的病人，在正式查体开始前就已经给了你重要信息。
生命体征很少应该被当成孤立数字看。频率、趋势、组合，以及它们和病人整体表现是否一致，都很重要。
查体必须保持目标导向。focused exam 先回答最紧急的问题，只有在病人和场景允许时才进一步做更详细的 exam。
体格 findings 本身有局限。看起来“还好”的 exam 不一定能排除危险，尤其是在会随时间进展的病情里。
keyPointsEn: A physical examination should produce interpretable information, not just completed actions. Every finding matters because it changes or confirms your clinical picture.

General impression often guides the tempo of the entire call. A patient who looks toxic, exhausted, confused, cyanotic, or obviously distressed is already telling you something important before the formal exam begins.
Vital signs should rarely be treated as isolated data points. Rate, trend, combination, and consistency with the rest of the patient all matter.
The exam must remain goal-directed. A focused exam answers the most urgent question first; a more detailed exam follows when the patient and situation allow.
Physical findings have limits. A normal-looking exam does not always exclude danger, especially in conditions that evolve over time.
mustKnowZh: 整体印象是真实的临床数据。
生命体征只有放进 context 里才有意义。
体格检查应该服务临床问题，而不是完成仪式。
mustKnowEn: General impression is real data.
Vital signs are only meaningful when interpreted in context.
The physical exam should answer clinical questions, not just complete a ritual.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：查得越多就越好。实际上相关性比数量更重要。
常见误区 2：一个生命体征正常就说明安全。实际上 context 和趋势更重要。
常见误区 3：体格检查可以单独成立。实际上它必须和病史、现场和 reassessment 一起用。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: more exam always means better exam. In reality, relevance matters more than volume.
Common confusion 2: a single normal vital sign means safety. In reality, context and trend matter more.
Common confusion 3: physical examination can stand alone. In reality, it must be integrated with history, scene, and reassessment.

reviews.summaryZh: 病人评估是把零散观察整合成有组织临床行动的框架。这一章教你 scene assessment、initial assessment、focused exam、detailed exam 和 ongoing reassessment 是怎么串起来的。重点不是背死顺序，而是建立一种稳定的思维方式，去处理那些优先级可能每分钟都在变化的病人。
reviews.summaryEn: Patient assessment is the framework that turns isolated observations into organized clinical action. This chapter teaches how scene assessment, initial assessment, focused and detailed examination, and ongoing reassessment fit together. The goal is not to memorize a rigid sequence, but to build a reliable way of thinking through patients whose priorities may change minute by minute.
highlightsZh: assessment 有结构，但也是动态的。
病人还没开口，scene 就已经在给你信息。
initial assessment 决定优先级和紧迫度。
focused assessment 和 detailed assessment 负责不同任务。
reassessment 是病人“开始暴露自己变化”的地方。
highlightsEn: Assessment is structured, but it is also dynamic.
The scene gives you information before the patient even speaks.
Initial assessment sets priority and urgency.
Focused assessment and detailed assessment have different jobs.
Reassessment is where changing patients declare themselves.
keyPointsZh: assessment 在真正接触病人前就已经开始。scene、机制、环境和可用资源都会提前塑造你的预期和优先级。
initial assessment 不只是第一步，它决定病人是稳定、不稳定，还是已经在走向失败。
focused assessment 是有方向、有选择的，它的存在是为了先回答最紧急的临床问题。
detailed assessment 是在时间、稳定度和场景允许时增加深度，但绝不能抢走优先级。
reassessment 不是为了机械重复，而是因为病人会变化，而变化中的病人常常会在时间里暴露更多真正的问题。
keyPointsEn: Assessment begins before direct patient contact. The scene, mechanism, environment, and available resources already shape your expectations and priorities.
The initial assessment is not just the first step—it is the step that decides whether the patient is stable, unstable, or actively failing.
A focused assessment is directed and selective. It exists to answer the most urgent clinical question first.
A detailed assessment adds depth when time, stability, and scene conditions allow, but it should never distract from priorities.
Reassessment is not repetition for its own sake. It exists because patients evolve, and evolving patients often become more informative over time.
mustKnowZh: assessment 是一种有结构的思维方式，不是冻结的台词。
scene 和 first impression 会影响后面的一切。
reassessment 是 EMS 最强的安全工具之一。
mustKnowEn: Assessment is a structured way of thinking, not a frozen script.
Scene and first impression influence everything that follows.
Reassessment is one of the strongest safety tools in EMS.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：每个病人都必须照完全相同顺序评估。实际上优先级会随着病情严重度变化。
常见误区 2：reassessment 只是为了文书。实际上它是识别恶化最主要的方式之一。
常见误区 3：scene assessment 只是安全检查。实际上它也会告诉你机制、紧迫度和可能的病理方向。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: every patient should be assessed in the same exact order. In reality, priorities change with acuity.
Common confusion 2: reassessment is only for documentation. In reality, it is one of the main ways worsening is recognized.
Common confusion 3: scene assessment is just about hazards. In reality, it also informs mechanism, urgency, and likely pathology.

reviews.summaryZh: 临床决策是把知识转化成行动的过程。这一章教你在信息不完整、时间有限的真实病人场景里，如何排序、解读、选择和行动。重点不是马上得到一个完美诊断，而是用手里已有的信息做出当前最安全、最有价值的下一步决定。
reviews.summaryEn: Clinical decisions are what turn knowledge into action. This chapter teaches how to prioritize, interpret, choose, and act in real patient situations where information is incomplete and time is limited. The goal is not to produce the perfect diagnosis immediately, but to make the safest and most useful next decision with the information available.
highlightsZh: 优先级往往比精确命名更早重要。
最可能诊断不一定等于最重要下一步。
protocol 引导决策，但不能代替判断。
临床推理核心在识别危险，而不只是贴标签。
把体征、病史和 context 合起来，决策才会更稳。
highlightsEn: Priority often matters before precision.
The most likely diagnosis is not always the most important next move.
Protocols guide decisions but do not replace judgment.
Clinical reasoning is about danger recognition, not just label assignment.
Decision-making improves when signs, history, and context are integrated.
keyPointsZh: 临床决策不只是给疾病命名，而是先识别此刻最威胁病人的是什么、最先必须做什么。
EMS 里的很多决定其实是 priority decision，而不是 final diagnosis decision，比如气道支持、转运时机、目的地选择和支持升级。
protocol 提供结构和安全边界，但不能机械替代临床思考。
好的决定建立在整合上：症状、生命体征、scene clues、病史、病程变化和可用资源都要放进来。
一个强的 clinician 不只问“这是什么病”，还会问“现在最危险的是什么”“正在变什么”“下一步最重要的是什么”。
keyPointsEn: Clinical decision-making is not just about naming disease. It is about recognizing what threatens the patient most right now and what must be done first.
Many decisions in EMS are priority decisions rather than final-diagnosis decisions. This includes airway support, transport timing, destination choice, and escalation of care.
Protocols provide structure and safety boundaries, but they are not mechanical substitutes for clinical thinking.
Good decisions are built from integration: symptoms, vital signs, scene clues, patient history, trajectory, and available resources all matter.
A strong clinician asks not only “What is this?” but also “What is dangerous?”, “What is changing?”, and “What matters most next?”
mustKnowZh: EMS 里的决策很多时候是在选最安全的下一步，而不是马上命中最完美诊断。
protocol 需要判断去应用。
识别危险是临床推理最核心的功能之一。
mustKnowEn: Decision-making in EMS is often about choosing the safest next step, not naming the perfect diagnosis.
Protocols need judgment to be applied well.
Danger recognition is one of the central functions of clinical reasoning.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：诊断总是第一目标。实际上第一目标常常是 stabilizing patient 和识别危险。
常见误区 2：有 protocol 就没有不确定性。实际上 protocol 仍然需要判断。
常见误区 3：临床推理只是复杂理论。实际上它很大一部分是有纪律的优先级排序。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: diagnosis is always the first goal. In reality, the first goal is often stabilizing the patient and identifying danger.
Common confusion 2: protocols eliminate uncertainty. In reality, protocols still require judgment.
Common confusion 3: clinical reasoning is complex theory. In reality, much of it is disciplined prioritization.

reviews.summaryZh: 沟通与记录保证你的照护不会在你手里结束。这一章教你如何清楚传达信息、有效交接病人，并把照护过程记录成能支持连续照护、病人安全、法律防御和系统学习的形式。重点不是单纯会说或会写，而是让你的 clinical work 能被别人看懂、接住、继续下去。
reviews.summaryEn: Communications and documentation ensure that care continues beyond the moment you provide it. This chapter teaches how to transmit clear information, hand over patients effectively, and document care in a way that supports continuity, safety, legal defensibility, and system learning. The goal is not only to speak or write, but to make your clinical work understandable and usable by others.
highlightsZh: 只有接手团队真正理解重点，沟通才算成功。
记录是病人照护的一部分，不只是法律防御。
交班时信息顺序和清晰度都很重要。
无线电沟通会影响接收准备和资源使用。
即使治疗做对了，差的沟通也可能造成伤害。
highlightsEn: Communication is successful only when the next team truly understands what matters.
Documentation is part of patient care, not just legal protection.
The order and clarity of handoff matter.
Radio communication affects preparation and resource use.
Poor communication can create harm even when the treatment itself was correct.
keyPointsZh: 沟通应该回答接手团队真正需要知道的事：发生了什么？病人有多重？你做了什么？病情有什么变化？接下来最要紧的是什么？
交班质量会直接影响连续照护。漏掉关键资料，可能导致重复劳动、治疗延误，或本可避免的错误。
documentation 不是把记得的东西全倒出来，而是要准确反映 findings、处理、时间中的变化，以及关键决策背后的理由。
无线电报告常常是接收系统第一次为病人做准备的机会，所以清楚和相关性非常关键。
如果 clinical work 没被清楚传达，很多时候就等于它没办法被正确接下去。
keyPointsEn: Communication should answer the receiving team’s real needs: What is happening? How sick is the patient? What have you done? What changed? What matters next?
Handoff quality affects continuity of care. Missing critical information may cause duplicated work, delayed treatment, or preventable mistakes.
Documentation is not a memory dump. It should accurately reflect findings, actions, change over time, and the reasoning behind key decisions.
Radio reports are often the receiving system’s first opportunity to prepare resources and personnel. This makes clarity and relevance essential.
Clinical work that is not clearly communicated is often clinical work that cannot be continued properly.
mustKnowZh: 交班要追求 clinical usefulness，而不是只是看起来完整。
记录要做到准确、清楚、站得住。
沟通失败本身就是 patient-safety 问题。
mustKnowEn: Handoff should be clinically useful, not just complete-looking.
Documentation should be accurate, clear, and defensible.
Communication failure is a patient-safety issue.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：只要我都讲了，沟通就算成功。实际上关键在于对方听懂了什么、记住了什么。
常见误区 2：documentation 主要是为了法律。实际上它同时也是连续照护和质量改进工具。
常见误区 3：交班只是形式。实际上它常常决定下一阶段照护能不能正确开始。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: if I said everything, communication was successful. In reality, what matters is what the other team understood and retained.
Common confusion 2: documentation exists mainly for legal reasons. In reality, it is also a continuity and quality tool.
Common confusion 3: handoff is just a formality. In reality, it may determine whether the next phase of care starts correctly.

reviews.titleZh: Chapter 20 — Trauma Systems and Mechanism of Injury
reviews.titleEn: Chapter 20 — Trauma Systems and Mechanism of Injury
reviews.summaryZh: 创伤系统与受伤机制，是 trauma care 开头最关键的推理框架。这一章教你的不是只看哪里有伤，而是要理解发生了什么、能量怎么传递、哪些结构更可能受损，以及病人应该被送到哪里接受 definitive care。真正的能力，是在伤势还没完全露出来之前就先推理出危险。
reviews.summaryEn: Trauma systems and mechanism of injury form the reasoning framework at the beginning of trauma care. This chapter teaches that trauma is not understood only by looking at obvious wounds, but by understanding what happened, how energy was transferred, what structures are likely involved, and where the patient should go for definitive care. The real skill is early injury prediction before the full picture becomes visible.
highlightsZh: 受伤机制是预测工具，不是装饰性背景。
能量传递往往在详细查体前就已经透露风险。
创伤目的地很重要，因为 definitive care 能力真的会改变结局。
看起来还稳定的 trauma 病人，也可能依然高风险。
trauma reasoning 在 head-to-toe exam 完成前就已经开始了。
highlightsEn: Mechanism of injury is predictive, not decorative background.
Energy transfer often reveals risk before anatomy is fully examined.
Trauma destination matters because definitive care capability matters.
A stable-looking trauma patient may still be high risk.
Trauma thinking begins before the head-to-toe exam is finished.
keyPointsZh: 受伤机制能帮助预测隐藏伤。病人的身体吸收能量的方式，常常不会马上在体表 exam 上完全显现。
力量越大、机制越复杂，就越要担心内伤、多系统创伤和延迟恶化。
创伤系统存在的意义，是把病人的需求和系统能力匹配起来。最好的目的地不一定是最近的，而是最能提供 definitive care 的那个。
scene clues、车体侵入、变形、速度、约束装置、抛出车外、坠落高度和身体受力姿态都很重要，因为它们帮助你估算病人的身体究竟承受了什么。
好的 trauma reasoning 通常从这几个问题开始：发生了什么？力量有多大？这种力量最可能伤到哪里？什么会先杀掉病人？
keyPointsEn: Mechanism of injury helps predict hidden damage. A patient’s body may absorb energy in ways that are not immediately visible from the surface exam.
The higher the force and the more complex the mechanism, the greater the concern for internal injury, multisystem trauma, and delayed deterioration.
Trauma systems exist to match patient need with system capability. The best destination is not always the closest destination—it is the one most able to provide definitive care.
Scene clues, intrusion, deformity, speed, restraints, ejection, falls, and body position all matter because they help estimate what the body experienced.
Good trauma reasoning starts with asking: What happened? What force was involved? What could that force have damaged? What will kill the patient first?
mustKnowZh: MOI 属于诊断和优先级判断的一部分。
创伤目的地决策会影响结局。
严重创伤不一定第一眼就明显。
现场信息本身就是临床信息。
mustKnowEn: MOI is part of diagnosis and priority-setting.
Trauma destination decisions affect outcome.
Not all serious trauma is obvious at first glance.
Scene information is clinical information.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：机制只是背景故事。实际上它常常在查体前就先告诉你危险在哪里。
常见误区 2：能走能说的 trauma 病人就一定低风险。实际上代偿常会暂时掩盖严重内伤。
常见误区 3：最近医院一定是正确目的地。实际上系统能力更重要。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: mechanism is just extra story. In reality, it often predicts danger before the exam does.
Common confusion 2: a walking or talking trauma patient must be low risk. In reality, compensation may temporarily hide serious injury.
Common confusion 3: the nearest hospital is always the right destination. In reality, system capability matters.

reviews.summaryZh: 出血与休克，是 trauma 里最致命的一条生理主线之一：失血导致灌注失败，再走向组织缺氧。这一章不只是讲看得见的出血，而是讲出血如何威胁循环、代偿如何掩盖严重度，以及为什么越早识别，结局越可能被改写。它训练你的，是在病人彻底崩掉前就把 shock 看出来。
reviews.summaryEn: Bleeding and shock teach one of the most lethal physiologic pathways in trauma: blood loss leading to perfusion failure and tissue hypoxia. This chapter is not just about visible bleeding. It is about how hemorrhage threatens circulation, how compensation can hide severity, and why early recognition changes outcome. The chapter trains you to recognize shock before the patient collapses completely.
highlightsZh: 休克在变成血压问题前，首先是灌注问题。
可见出血只是危险的一部分。
代偿性休克很会骗人。
骨盆、腹部和胸腔出血，即使没有夸张体表表现，也可能非常严重。
越早识别，后面的处理和目的地选择就越容易改写结局。
highlightsEn: Shock is a perfusion problem before it becomes a blood-pressure problem.
Visible bleeding is only part of the danger.
Compensated shock is deceptive.
Pelvic, abdominal, and thoracic bleeding may be major even without dramatic external signs.
Early recognition changes everything from treatment to destination choice.
keyPointsZh: 出血之所以危险，是因为血液承担氧输送和灌注维持。一旦丢失到一定程度，即使外表看起来不算夸张，器官也可能已经得不到足够供氧。
休克更准确地说是组织灌注失败，而不只是低血压。等到明显低血压时，病人往往已经进入比较深的生理失代偿。
代偿会让病人在一段时间里看起来还勉强“过得去”。所以皮肤表现、脉搏质量、意识状态、毛细血管再充盈、焦虑和整体外观都很重要。
隐匿性出血是 trauma 最大的坑之一。血液可以丢在胸腔、腹腔、骨盆和长骨周围，而病人体表却还看起来相对可控。
处理从识别和止血开始，但也包括目的地思维、reassessment，以及对病情可能快速恶化的预期。
keyPointsEn: Hemorrhage matters because blood carries oxygen and supports perfusion. Once enough blood volume is lost, organs no longer receive what they need even if external bleeding does not look dramatic.
Shock is better understood as failed tissue perfusion than as low blood pressure alone. By the time hypotension is obvious, the patient may already be deep into physiologic compromise.
Compensation can maintain a superficially acceptable appearance for a while. This is why skin signs, pulse quality, mentation, capillary refill, anxiety, and overall appearance all matter.
Hidden bleeding is one of the major trauma traps. Blood can be lost into the chest, abdomen, pelvis, and long bones while the outside of the patient still looks relatively controlled.
Management begins with recognition and hemorrhage control, but it also includes destination thinking, reassessment, and understanding that deterioration may happen quickly.
mustKnowZh: 要尽早想灌注，不要等到晚期才盯血压。
没有夸张外出血，绝不能让你过度放心。
代偿会暂时掩盖致命休克。
休克会演变，所以 reassessment 非常关键。
mustKnowEn: Think perfusion early, not just blood pressure late.
Never let the absence of dramatic external bleeding reassure you too much.
Compensation can temporarily hide life-threatening shock.
Reassessment is critical because shock evolves.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：血压还行，就没有休克。实际上病人可能还在代偿。
常见误区 2：外面看不到大出血，就说明问题不大。实际上隐匿性出血可能非常致命。
常见误区 3：休克识别主要靠 monitor。实际上很多时候病人先通过外观和灌注线索告诉你。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: if blood pressure is okay, there is no shock. In reality, compensation may still be active.
Common confusion 2: if the bleeding is not obvious externally, it is probably minor. In reality, hidden bleeding can be catastrophic.
Common confusion 3: shock recognition belongs to the monitor. In reality, the patient often tells you first through appearance and perfusion clues.

reviews.summaryZh: 软组织损伤看起来常像“只是伤口”，但它背后可能隐藏大量失血、深层组织损伤、污染、感染风险、挤压伤生理变化，以及爆炸伤带来的复杂机制。这一章的重点，是让学生不再把 wound 当成单纯皮肤问题，而是把 soft-tissue injury 当成与机制、出血、组织保护和全身风险都相关的伤。真正目标是准确判断严重度、先控制当前威胁，再避免现场处理本身把组织伤得更重。
reviews.summaryEn: Soft-tissue injuries may look simple on the surface, but they can hide major bleeding, deep structural damage, contamination, infection risk, crush physiology, and blast-related complexity. This chapter teaches students to stop thinking of wounds as skin problems and start thinking about tissue injury as a whole-body and mechanism-linked problem. The goal is to recognize severity accurately, control immediate threats, and protect injured tissue from becoming more damaged during field care.
highlightsZh: 软组织伤不只是皮肤问题。
体表外观常会低估内部严重度。
止血和组织保护是核心优先级。
挤压伤和爆炸伤往往隐藏复杂损伤。
伤口处理不是把它盖住而已，而是生理与损伤控制问题。
highlightsEn: Soft-tissue injury is more than a skin problem.
External appearance may underestimate internal severity.
Bleeding control and tissue protection are core priorities.
Crush and blast injuries often involve hidden complexity.
Wound care is about physiology and damage control, not just covering the wound.
keyPointsZh: 开放性和闭合性软组织损伤都可能很危险。开放伤看起来明显，但闭合伤同样可能隐藏严重出血、肿胀、间隔室风险或深层组织破坏。
伤口评估不能只看表面，要同时看出血程度、组织暴露、污染、疼痛、功能、机制，以及是否合并血管、神经或肌骨损伤。
止血常常是第一优先级，因为一旦出血持续，问题就会从局部组织伤快速变成全身性休克问题。压力、敷料、适当时的 wound packing 和持续 reassessment 都属于真正有意义的 wound care。
挤压伤危险，是因为外面看起来可能没那么夸张，但里面的肌肉破坏、肿胀、血管受压和后续全身并发问题可能非常重。provider 必须学会超越外观去想。
爆炸伤很少只是一个伤口。它往往同时包括压力波伤、碎片穿透伤、烧伤、抛掷伤和隐藏内伤，所以机制本身就该让你想到多系统损伤。
软组织处理的目的不是为了把 wound 弄得好看，而是为了止血、保护暴露组织、降低感染风险、减少搬运中的进一步损伤，并在转运途中持续保护组织。
keyPointsEn: Open and closed soft-tissue injuries can both be dangerous. A dramatic open wound may be obvious, but a closed soft-tissue injury can still hide severe bleeding, swelling, compartment concerns, or deep tissue damage.
Wound assessment should include bleeding severity, tissue exposure, contamination, pain, function, mechanism, and the possibility of associated vascular, neurologic, or musculoskeletal injury.

Bleeding control is often the first priority because blood loss can rapidly shift the problem from local tissue injury to systemic shock. Pressure, dressings, packing when appropriate, and constant reassessment are all part of meaningful wound care.

Crush injuries are dangerous because the visible external injury may underestimate internal muscle destruction, swelling, vascular compromise, and later systemic complications. The field provider must think beyond appearance.

Blast injuries are rarely just a wound. They may involve pressure effects, penetrating fragments, burns, displacement injury, and hidden internal trauma. The mechanism itself raises suspicion for multi-system injury.

The purpose of soft-tissue management is not cosmetic cleanup. It is hemorrhage control, protection of exposed or damaged tissue, infection-risk reduction, pain-aware handling, and prevention of further injury during transport.
mustKnowZh: 绝不要只凭皮肤外观判断软组织伤的严重度。
很多时候止血比 wound 细节更优先。
挤压伤和爆炸伤必须按机制去思考。
任何 wound care 后都要 reassess。
mustKnowEn: Never judge soft-tissue severity by skin appearance alone.
Bleeding control often matters before wound detail.
Crush and blast injuries require mechanism-based thinking.
Wound care should always include reassessment.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：伤口看起来小，损伤就一定小。实际上深度和机制可能让它非常严重。
常见误区 2：wound care 主要就是包扎。实际上核心是止血、组织保护和风险识别。
常见误区 3：闭合性软组织伤一定比开放伤风险低。实际上隐藏出血和肿胀可能同样严重。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: if the wound looks small, the injury must be small. In reality, depth and mechanism may make it severe.
Common confusion 2: wound care is mostly about bandaging. In reality, it is about hemorrhage control, tissue protection, and risk recognition.
Common confusion 3: closed soft-tissue injury is automatically lower risk than open injury. In reality, hidden bleeding and swelling can be serious.

reviews.summaryZh: 烧伤不是单纯的皮肤受伤，它是热、化学、电或辐射造成的损伤，可能同时打乱液体平衡、灌注、疼痛控制、感染风险、气道状态和整体生理。这一章训练学生超越看见的烧伤面积，去理解深度、范围、部位、机制、吸入伤风险和全身后果。真正目标是分清：谁只是局部损伤，谁已经开始全身不稳，谁需要 burn center 等更高级资源。
reviews.summaryEn: Burns are not simply skin injuries—they are thermal, chemical, electrical, or radiation-related injuries that may disrupt fluid balance, perfusion, pain control, infection risk, airway status, and overall physiology. This chapter teaches students to move beyond the visible burn and think in terms of depth, extent, location, mechanism, inhalation risk, and systemic consequence. The goal is to recognize who can be managed simply, who is physiologically unstable, and who requires specialized burn-focused care.
highlightsZh: 烧伤是全身性问题，不只是皮肤问题。
深度、范围、部位和吸入伤风险都重要。
气道受累有时比肉眼可见烧伤更危险。
严重烧伤必须加入液体与灌注思维。
烧伤严重度靠生理判断，不只靠外观。
highlightsEn: Burns are systemic problems, not just skin problems.
Depth, extent, location, and inhalation risk all matter.
Airway involvement may be more dangerous than the visible burn.
Fluid and perfusion thinking are essential in serious burns.
Burn severity is judged by physiology, not appearance alone.
keyPointsZh: 烧伤评估首先从机制开始。热烧伤、化学烧伤、电烧伤和辐射烧伤的行为并不一样，每一种都会造成不同的局部和全身损伤模式。
深度重要，因为浅表、部分层和全层烧伤在组织后果和愈合意义上完全不同。范围也重要，因为烧伤越大，就越可能引发全身不稳。
部位非常重要。面部、气道、手、足、会阴和大关节附近的烧伤，常会带来单看面积看不出来的严重并发问题。
对烧伤病人的 airway assessment 非常关键，尤其当存在吸入伤怀疑、面部烧伤、密闭空间暴露、烟灰、声音嘶哑或进行性肿胀时。外观看起来不算很大的烧伤，依然可能变成气道急症。
大面积或重度烧伤会造成明显液体转移、灌注问题、热量丢失、剧痛和感染脆弱性。所以严重烧伤必须按 whole-patient thinking 处理，而不是只把它当 wound。
EMS 早期 burn care 的目标，是阻止持续损伤、保护病人、减少热量丢失与污染、监测气道和休克问题，并识别谁需要 specialized transport 或更高级资源。
keyPointsEn: Burn assessment starts with mechanism. Thermal, chemical, electrical, and radiation burns do not all behave the same way, and each mechanism creates different patterns of local and systemic injury.

Depth matters because superficial, partial-thickness, and full-thickness injuries do not carry the same tissue consequences or healing implications. Extent matters because larger burns are more likely to cause systemic instability.
Location matters greatly. Burns involving the face, airway, hands, feet, genitalia, or major joints may create complications that are not fully captured by total body surface area alone.

Airway assessment is critical in burn patients, especially when there is suspicion of inhalation injury, facial burns, enclosed-space exposure, soot, hoarseness, or progressive swelling. A visually modest burn can still become an airway emergency.

Large or severe burns can cause major fluid shifts, perfusion problems, heat loss, pain, and infection vulnerability. Serious burn care therefore requires whole-patient thinking, not wound-only thinking.

The purpose of early burn care in EMS is to stop ongoing injury, protect the patient, limit heat loss and contamination, monitor for airway and shock problems, and identify who needs specialized transport or higher-level resources.
mustKnowZh: 不要只凭外观看烧伤严重度。
气道风险有时比可见烧伤更重要。
大面积或深烧伤属于全身性急症。
很多时候部位和面积同样重要。
mustKnowEn: Do not judge burn severity by surface appearance alone.
Airway risk can outweigh visible burn severity.
Large or deep burns are systemic emergencies.
Location matters as much as size in many cases.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：烧伤严重度就是看皮肤看起来多惨。实际上生理状态和气道风险可能更重要。
常见误区 2：只要算面积就够了。实际上深度和部位同样关键。
常见误区 3：看起来局部，问题就一定局部。实际上大烧伤是 whole-patient problem。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: burn severity equals what the skin looks like. In reality, physiology and airway risk may matter more.
Common confusion 2: total body surface area alone determines seriousness. In reality, depth and location are also critical.
Common confusion 3: if the burn looks local, the problem is local. In reality, major burns are whole-patient problems.

reviews.summaryZh: 头面部损伤绝不是外观问题而已。这类损伤可能同时牵涉大脑、气道、出血、眼睛、口腔、前颈部以及多种神经功能。这一章训练学生超越看起来很吓人的表面，去思考 airway compromise、创伤性脑损伤、感觉器官受损、肿胀，以及正在变化的 neurologic status。真正目标是：不要被外观带偏，却又能及时抓住最先致命的风险。
reviews.summaryEn: Head and face injuries demand more than cosmetic attention. These injuries may involve the brain, airway, bleeding, the eyes, the mouth, the anterior neck, and multiple neurologic functions at the same time. This chapter teaches students how to see beyond the visible injury and think about airway compromise, traumatic brain injury, sensory loss, swelling, and evolving neurologic change. The goal is to avoid being distracted by dramatic appearance while still recognizing what may become life-threatening first.
highlightsZh: 头面部损伤可能同时威胁气道、脑功能和循环。
外观看起来很吓人，但优先级仍然要回到 airway、neurologic status 和 perfusion。
面部出血和肿胀可能很快制造气道问题。
神经变化比看起来多惨更重要。
眼、口、耳和前颈部损伤都会提供重要临床信息。
highlightsEn: Head and face injury can threaten airway, brain function, and circulation at the same time.
Appearance may be dramatic, but priority still belongs to airway, neurologic status, and perfusion.
Facial bleeding and swelling can rapidly create airway problems.
Neurologic change matters more than external drama.
Eye, mouth, ear, and anterior neck injuries all add important clinical information.
keyPointsZh: 头部损伤评估绝不只是看表面伤口。更大的问题是：脑有没有受伤、神经功能有没有变化、病人是不是越来越失去保护 airway 的能力。
面部损伤特别危险，是因为它可能大量出血、迅速肿胀、扭曲正常解剖，并影响气道通畅。所以 face injury 很容易从局部伤变成 airway problem。
头部创伤里的 neurologic assessment 要注意意识状态、反应性、必要时的瞳孔发现、运动、语言，以及时间中的变化。趋势很重要，因为恶化不一定会立刻出现。
眼、耳、口腔和前颈部不是附属细节，它们常常能揭示受伤机制、严重度、功能损失、气道风险，以及会改变处理策略的相关损伤。
这一章训练的是有纪律的优先级思维：不要被血多、脸部很惨这些外观拖走注意力，而忽略真正先致命的东西——airway、ventilation、perfusion 和 brain function。
keyPointsEn: Head injury assessment is not just about visible wounds. The bigger concern is whether the brain has been injured, whether neurologic function is changing, and whether the patient is becoming less able to protect the airway.

Facial injuries create special danger because they may bleed heavily, swell rapidly, distort normal anatomy, and compromise airway patency. The face can therefore become an airway problem even when the initial complaint seems local.

Neurologic assessment in head trauma requires attention to mental status, responsiveness, pupil findings when appropriate, movement, speech, and change over time. Trend matters because deterioration may not be immediate.
The eyes, ears, mouth, and anterior neck are not extra details. They may reveal mechanism, severity, functional loss, airway risk, and associated trauma that changes management.
The chapter teaches disciplined priority thinking: do not let dramatic blood or visible facial damage distract you from the actual life threats—airway, ventilation, perfusion, and brain function.
mustKnowZh: 头面部损伤首先不是 cosmetic problem。
airway 和 neurologic status 往往才是真正优先级。
面部肿胀和出血可能会迅速变坏。
意识状态趋势非常重要。
mustKnowEn: Head and face injuries are not primarily cosmetic problems.
Airway and neurologic status are often the true priorities.
Facial swelling and bleeding may rapidly worsen.
Trend in mental status matters.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：外观看起来越惨，就说明最重要的问题就是外观最惨的地方。实际上可见伤常会分散你对更深危险的注意。
常见误区 2：面部创伤主要是局部创伤。实际上它很可能演变成 airway crisis。
常见误区 3：病人还醒着，神经危险就低。实际上恶化可能仍在进行中。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: dramatic appearance equals the most important problem. In reality, visible damage may distract from deeper danger.
Common confusion 2: facial trauma is mainly local trauma. In reality, it may become an airway crisis.
Common confusion 3: if the patient is still awake, neurologic danger is low. In reality, deterioration may still be evolving.

reviews.summaryZh: 脊柱损伤之所以危险，不只是因为骨头受伤，而是因为脊柱损伤可能直接威胁神经功能、长期活动能力，甚至影响病人维持生命的能力。这一章教你如何识别可能的脊柱损伤，如何根据受伤机制和神经体征提高警觉，以及为什么病人的移动本身就可能造成进一步伤害。核心不是为了固定而固定，而是通过有纪律的 handling 去保护神经功能。
reviews.summaryEn: Spine injuries are dangerous not only because the spine can be hurt, but because spinal injury can threaten neurologic function, long-term mobility, and the patient’s ability to protect life-sustaining systems. This chapter teaches how to recognize possible spinal injury, how mechanism and neurologic findings influence suspicion, and how movement itself can become harmful if not controlled. The goal is not rigid immobilization for its own sake, but protection of neurologic function through disciplined handling.
highlightsZh: 脊柱损伤评估本质上是看神经风险，不只是看背痛。
机制和移动方式都很关键。
保护神经比形式化固定更重要。
搬运决策本身会改变结局。
神经体征的权重非常高。
highlightsEn: Spinal injury assessment is about neurologic risk, not just back pain.
Mechanism and movement matter.
Protection is more important than ritual immobilization.
Handling decisions can change outcome.
Neurologic findings carry major weight.
keyPointsZh: 只要机制、症状、神经 findings、意识状态或整体 trauma context 提示脊柱或脊髓可能受伤，就应该提高对 spinal injury 的怀疑。
脊柱损伤最危险的地方不是疼痛本身，而是在于如果病人被不加计划地移动，神经功能可能进一步受损。
单纯背痛不是唯一重点。无力、麻木、感觉异常、功能下降，或高风险机制，往往比疼痛本身更有意义。
移动不是中性的。在可能不稳定的脊柱里，不必要的 movement 会加重损伤，所以“怎么移动”几乎和“要不要移动”同样重要。
这一章训练的是风险导向的脊柱管理，而不是无脑自动固定。provider 要结合机制、查体、功能状态，以及在照护和转运中如何最安全地维持稳定。
keyPointsEn: Spinal injury should be suspected when the mechanism, symptoms, neurologic findings, mental status, or overall trauma context suggest potential injury to the spine or spinal cord.
The danger in spine injury is not simply pain, but the possibility of further neurologic damage if the patient is moved carelessly or handled without a plan.
Back pain alone is not the defining issue. Weakness, numbness, altered sensation, loss of function, or concerning mechanism may carry more meaning than pain itself.
Movement is not neutral. In a potentially unstable spine, unnecessary movement can worsen injury. This is why how you move the patient matters as much as whether you move them.
